description: A mass meeting sponsored by the local chamber of commerce where citizens expressed determination to bring justice for a lynching victim, with rewards offered and resolutions adopted calling for action by authorities.
participants: Citizens, leading citizens, several ministers
source_text: Hattiesburg, Miss. — Determination to bring to Justice the brute who, lynched Emanuel Metcalf, an automobile mechanic, is expressed by citizens after a mass meeting sponsored by the local chamber of commerce. Rewards were offered and resolutions adopted urging that the authorities take every action possible, after leading citizens, including several ministers, had denounced the lynch-murders.

description: Over 100 women workers at Bayuk Brothers cigar factory walked out in protest against miserable working conditions and inadequate wages.
location: Philadelphia, Pa. (Bayuk Brothers factory, corner Ninth St. and Columbia Ave.)
participants: Over 100 unorganized women workers at Bayuk Brothers cigar factory
source_text: Philadelphia, Pa.—Over 100 of our unorganized women-workers at the world's largest cigar factory, Bayuk Brothers, corner Ninth St. and Columbia Ave., this city, walked out, a few weeks ago, in protest against the miserable conditions they had to work under. Given a rate of 25 cents for stripping 12 and a half pounds of tobacco, the pay envelopes of the group for a full week ranged from $3.50 to $11, the average being around $9 a week.

Jacksonville, Fla., July 25. The Avery law for the separation of the races was declared unconstitutional to-day, in the decision of a case prosecuted by Attorney J. Douglas Wetmore.

Memphis, Tenn., July 30. The Negroes of Memphis, at a meeting held to-day, raised a fund of $5000 with which to fight the "Jim Crow" law. Few Negroes are riding on the street cars and they say they will fight the case through the Supreme Court.

description: Negro Baptist organizations relocated their state convention from Sulphur Springs to Dallas in response to a recent lynching and burning of a Negro, effectively boycotting the unsafe location.

participants: Negro Baptist Sunday school and Baptist Young People's union state convention executive board
source_text: Dallas, Texas., Aug. 14. Because of the recent lynching at Sulphur Springs, Tex., the Negro Baptist Sunday school and Baptist Young People's union state convention will be held here instead of at Sulphur Springs. This descision was reached at a meeting of the executive board of the two bodies held here today. The statement was made that Sulphur Springs was not a safe place for Negroes at present, because of the feeling engendered by the burning of a Negro there a few days ago.

source_text:
Baltimore, Md., Jan 31.—The placing of separate waiting rooms for Negroes in the Camden station of the Baltimore & Ohio Railroad has aroused the colored people of this city as they have not been stirred lately.

The "Jim Crow" signs were placed in the station ten days ago, and the news spread like wildfire.

The pastors of various churches spoke out against the attempt of the road to "Jim Crow" the people of Baltimore. In their pulpits Sunday, and on Monday the colored Baptist Ministers' Confere

source_text: Louisville, Ky.-The members of the Negro Outlook Committee are making a fight for additional playgrounds for colored children of the city. Representatives of the committee petitioned the Park Board a few days ago to equip the lot at 16th and Baxter streeta, and to provide additional facilities to those in Baxter Square.

source_text:
GATHERED FROM VARIOUS SOURCES

Little Rock, Ark., Sept. 10.—Incomplete returns here indicate for a certainty that State-wide prohibition was defeated in yesterday's election. The "grandfathers" clause amendment, seeking to enact a requirement that a man's right to the ballot depended upon whether or not his grandfather had been able to read and write, was decisively beaten. This amendment was designed to disfranchise the Negro.

participants: Knights of Moses (2,000 Negro ministers and bishops)

Among the membership of the Knights of Moses are two thousand Negro ministers and bishops, and they hope to wipe off the statute books all laws which they consider to discriminate against their race, the Jim Crow law being one of their objects of attack.

description: A committee of five white persons and five Negroes issued a statement protesting against a proposed segregation ordinance in St. Louis.
participants: Committee of five white persons and five Negroes appointed by the St. Louis League
source_text:
OPPOSE SEGREGATION
St. Louis, Mo., Feb 1.—A committee of five white persons and five Negroes appointed by the St. Louis League to investigate the housing of Negroes, has issued a atstement protesting against a proposed ordinance segregating the Negroes of St. Louis. A bill to the effect is pending in the municipal assembly.

description: Illinois Black communities successfully lobbied against the Campbell Bill, which would have mandated racial segregation on railroad lines, resulting in the bill being killed in Senate Committee.
participants: Negroes of Illinois
source_text: Chicago, Ill., April 22.—The Negroes of Illinois scored their first victory against "Jim Crowism" in the State last week when succeeded in having the Campbell Bill killed in Senate Committee on Railroads. The Campbell Bill provides for the separation of races on railroad lines. The Negroes of Illinois made an aggressive fight against the passage of the Campbell Bill, and are as equally determined that all other unfriendly measures meet the same fate as the Campbell Bill.

description: White railway mail clerks submitted a petition with over 8,000 signatures to the Postmaster General demanding segregation of white and colored clerks in the railway mail service.
participants: Robert A. Prather and white railway mail clerks
source_text: Washington, D. C.—Robert A. Prather, of Little Rock, Ark., has reached here with the long-looked-for petition signed by more than 8,000 white railway mail clerks, asking an order segregating white and colored clerks in the railway mail service. The petition says that from fifteen to twenty clerks work in the same car; that the white clerks are often outnumbered by Negroes, sometimes subject to their authority, and on long runs have to share sleeping quarters with them. A movement is on foot to bring out an amalgamation of Republicans and Progressives, and committees looking toward that end have been appointed. description: Colored women workers at Butler Bros. wholesale house walked out in protest against being forced to use freight elevators instead of passenger elevators.
participants: Colored women workers (all except one)
source_text: All of the colored girls, with the exception of one, quit work at the Butler Bros. wholesale house. It is stated that the walkout was caused by refusing the colored women passage on the passenger elevators. Several of the women worked on the 8th floor and it is stated a ruling was made that they would have to ride on the freight elevator in the future. So rather than have themselves classed as freight they all quit work, with the exception of one. Dallas Express.

description: White women petitioned the governor for executive clemency for a Negro man convicted of assault, marking the first time such a recommendation was made in the state's prison commission history.
participants: Well-known white women, the victim of the alleged offense
source_text: Atlanta, Ga., Sept. 1.—For the first time in the history of the State prison commission, a large number of well known white women have recommended executive clemency for a Negro charged with an offense against a white woman. Lige Lane was convicted in Clinch county for assault. He was sentenced to hang, and unless the governor interferes, will be executed on September 10 A letter written by the Negro's victim, which also requested clemency, was submitted.

participants: Miners and their union representatives
source_text: The Southern coal fields of Colorado are in the midst of a big strike, the full extent of which is hard to determine on account of the extremely different figures given out by the operators or mine owners and the representatives of the miners union. The figures of either, however clearly indicate that this is the biggest strike Colorado has had for many years and is very likely to assume larger proportions in the very near future by miners in other districts going on a sympathetic strike.

source_text:
A remarkable editorial appeared in the "Jewish Daily News," published in New York City, on Tuesday, July 31st. The article, which was captioned, "No Room for Race Prejudice," was occasioned by the great silent protest parade which was held in New York following the East St. Louis riots and was participated in by eight thousand Negroes. It was a masterful plea for justice, and says in part:

"Pitiful wrongs and grave acts of injustice have been and are continued to be committed against the Negro

Every phase of the Race condition of the United States will be discussed, including the East St. Louis, the Chester, Pa., riots and the Immigration of Colored People North. description: A three-day Negro race congress in New York with over 200 participants that included petitioning President Wilson on lynching and racial discrimination, and discussing a planned march to Washington.
participants: More than 200 Negroes from all parts of the country; Rev. Byron Gunner (president), William Monroe Trotter (secretary)
source_text: New York, Sept. I8.—More than 200 Negroes from all parts of the country, met here today for the Negro race congress, which is to last three days. The Rev. Byron Gunner was elected president and William Monroe Trotter secretary of the congress. The program included a petition to President Wilson to make lynching a federal crime, to evolve means to stop alleged unjust discrimination against Negroes, and to discuss a possible march to Washington.

source_text:
Memphis, Tenn., Sept. 21. Colored Association of Railway Employees of this city ordered its members who are employed as switchmen for the Illinois Central here to walk out because the officials refused the men a 25 cents raise per day. The white union assisted in preventing scabs to mount the engines. Pat Gilliam was the only member to lower the effort by scabbing. The men won.

description: Members of the 24th Infantry refused to obey segregation laws in Texas, challenging Jim Crow regulations by refusing to comply with racial separation signs.
participants: Members of the 24th Infantry
source_text: This trouble started by the members of the 24th infantry refusing to obey the laws of Texas and other southern states. You people who are here from the South can appreciate what I say that in different states of the South ehere are jim-crow laws that must be adhered to. So, here in Houston, Texas, is the law. In different places there were posted signs, some "For White" and others "For Negroes."

description: Members of the 24th Infantry engaged in collective defiance of segregation laws by removing segregation signs, sitting in segregated spaces, and refusing to obey segregation regulations in Texas.
participants: Members of the 24th Infantry
source_text:
Members of the 24th infantry went around with chips on their shoulders, pulling down these signs, sitting in parks, cars and theaters where these signs of segregations were and refusing to adhere to them. They objected to the rules of segregation, drank out of water barrels that were marked for white soldiers only and overturned the water barrels marked for Negroes.

description: An uprising by men in response to the beating of Corporal Baltimore and the arrest of a policeman on murder charges.
participants: Men (presumably soldiers or military personnel based on reference to Corporal)
source_text: The direct cause of the trouble was the beating of Corporal Baltimore. A policeman is arrested and under indictment of murder. The direct cause was that the men had a chip on their shoulder and the uprising would have taken place anywhere and at any other place.

description: The Negro Silent Protest Parade was held in Providence, Rhode Island, with B. T. Montgomery serving as marshal.
participants: African American organizers and participants; B. T. Montgomery (marshal)
source_text: Providence. R. I.—B. T. Montgomery, marshal of the Negro Silent Protest Parade, which was held in this city recently, was interviewed by a Federal investigator from the Department of Justice. The object was to ascertain whether or not German influence was back of the parade. The marshal emphatically denied any hint of German money having been given to finance the parade. He informed the investigator that all money was given solely by colored people.

description: Twenty-five hundred workers at Toledo Shipbuilding company struck in protest against the company's increase in Negro workers from 15 to 40.

source_text: Toledo, Ohio, April 23.—Twenty-five hundred men employed in the Toledo Shipbuilding company yards struck today, charging that the company had increased the number of its Negro workers from 15 to 40. The company, which still has 15 vessels to build for the government, is practically closed down, officials stated. It is planned to refer the dispute to the federal shipping board.

description: A conference formally expressed opposition to discriminatory practices by railroads and common carriers against African Americans regarding accommodations and fares.
participants: Conference attendees/organization
source_text: The conference wishes to re-affirm its opposition to the discrimination practiced against Negroes by railroads and other common carriers. This discrimination is unnecessary and unfair. There is an indefensible injustice in charging Negroes first-class fares and providing them with third and fourth-class accommodations.

E. ministers' meeting Monday. Bishop Hurst returned from visits to the A. M. E. conferences in Florida.

description: One hundred fifty colored workers struck at the Hooven, Owen, Rentschler Engineer plant in Hamilton, Ohio to protest racial segregation of workplace toilets, successfully forcing management to remove Jim Crow restrictions.
participants: One hundred fifty colored workmen at Hooven, Owen, Rentschler Engineer plant
source_text:
LABORERS ON STRIKE; RETURN TO WORK.

After "Jim Crowism" Is Removed by Employers.

Hamilton, O., March 24.—The one hundred and fifty colored workmen who went on a strike here last week because of "Jim Crowism" at the Hooven, Owen, Rentchler Engineer plant of the company's toilets, returned to work today after the management had agreed to lift the ban on "Jim Crowing." The colored men struck when an order prohibiting use of same toilets by whites and blacks.

New Orleans, March 17.—More than seventy-five colored pastors, half the number in attendance at the Inter-Church World Movement meeting, being held in the First Presbyterian Church, left the assembly room late yesterday when they were refused permission to sit in the same section as the white ministers. The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, 70 Fifth avenue, New York, today announced a final speaking tour by its secretary, James Weldon Johnson, early in November, before the special session of Congress at which action will be had on the Dyer Anti-Lynching Bill.

description: Organized political campaign by colored voters and the NAACP against congressmen who voted against the Dyer Anti-Lynching Bill, including organizing meetings and mobilizing voters to defeat them in elections.
participants: Delaware colored voters, Mrs. Alice Dumbar Nelson, NAACP speakers, newspaper editors

Two congressmen who voted against the Dyer Anti-Lynching Bill in the House of Representatives have been retired by colored votes and defeated in their candidacy for re-election, according to announcement today by the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People, 70 Fifth Avenue, New York. The two congressmen are: Dr. Caleb R. Layton, Republican representative at large from the state of Delaware, and R. Wayne Parker, Republican representative from the Ninth New Jersey district.

Washington, D. C., July 19.—The active and organized protests of the colored men and women of the country, backed by numerous other organizations, have won the day in the fight to eliminate the color clause from the proposed national marriage and divorce bill.

participants: French Negro population

French Prohibit Showing of Vicious Film as Negroes Show Resentment

Paris, Aug. 31.—Because feelings of French Negroes have been outraged by the barbarities of Americans, who resented their presence in restaurants and bars the showing of D. W. Griffith's film, "The Birth of a Nation," has been prohibited by the police.

The censor passed on the picture favorably twelve days ago, but it is understood the police ban has been clapped on as a concession to France's Negro population. Premier Poincare

description: A league committee called on the City Manager and County Commission to protest against a racist movie play, resulting in an ordinance prohibiting indecent plays and those creating race hatred.
participants: League committee members representing the race
source_text:
Phoenix, Ariz.—The first work achieved by the league organized to protect the right and interest of the race in this city was the suppression of the vicious movie play, "The Nigger."

A committee called on the City Manager and the County Commission in protest, and as a result an ordinance was enacted prohibiting all plays that are indecent or that in way tend to create race hatred.

As none of them had seen it he requested them to do so and then return to him, before he would agree to take any action. description: Colored people brought charges against improper voter registration and initiated an investigation into the matter.
participants: Colored people (African Americans)
source_text: Many colored men living in one district in Kansas City were registsred from another and so were unable to vote. This has been explained as a clerical error. The colored people have brought charges and an investigation is being made.

description: Colored citizens of Roslyn, Long Island are organizing a collective action to demand the closure of a segregated school established for their children.
participants: Colored citizens of Roslyn
source_text: The colored citizens of Roslyn, L. L., are making a determined fight to have a small school set apart for their children closed.

description: Legal suits were filed against Bethlehem Steel to challenge racial discrimination and secure civil rights for African Americans in Huntington.
participants: Mr. Squires and other African Americans experiencing discrimination
source_text: The fact that business men in Huntington were inclined to discriminate against Negro patronage was brought out by Mr. Squires, who stated that deliberate insults were tendered Negro patrons in various places, in one place a drinking glass being smashed in front of the colored customer, after the men had finished an ice cream soda. The suits filed against Bethlemen are for the purpose of securing civil rights and to put a stop to race discrimination in this village.

description: Dr. Ransom calls for Black Americans to demand the establishment of a separate Colored regiment with Colored officers as a condition for military service.

source_text: "When it comes to fighting for this country," said Dr. Ransom, "I counsel the black people, who are 10,000,000 strong, to demand that there is to be a separate Colored regiment, there shall be appointed Colored officers to direct it, and on that principle demand certain conditions under which they will fight the country's battles. But let Americans of African descent remember that if they don't fight for the Stars and Stripes they have no other flag to fight for."

The protest includes not only the enlisted men docketed for trial, but several hundred of their comrades.

description: White railroad workers at Scullin-Galagher's Steel Plant went on strike for higher wages around February 1, 1917, and were temporarily replaced by Black workers from the South before being rehired.
participants: White crew engineers, firemen, and brakemen; Black workers from the South
source_text: Colored men engineers, firemen and brakemen are handling the switch engines and cars at Scullin-Galagher's Steel Plant of this city. According to information, about February 1 the white crew went on a strike for higher wages. Their places were filled temporarily by Colored men who came from the South, who had some experience along these lines. For fear that the railroad company would refuse to furnish cars, the white men were put back on the job, with continued dissatisfaction to all concerned.

source_text: Two hundred striking Negro miners at the Banner Mines near Birmingham, Ala. have refused to come out of the mines or to allow anyone to enter. The men are convicts and have quantities of dynamite. They complain of ill treatment.

source_text: New York, July 28.—As a protest against the recent E. St. Louis race riots, Negro residents of New York have arranged for a demonstration today, to take the form of a silent parade down Fifth Avenue. It was announced that more than 2,000 had signified their intention to join in the demonstration.

description: A call for delegates from organizations nationwide to attend the tenth annual convention of the National Equal Rights League and Second National Congress of All Colored Americans to demand equal rights as citizens.
participants: Churches, lodges, business and fraternal societies, and the entire Colored American population
source_text: All organizations--churches lodges, business and fraternal societies—among us, through out these United States, are cordially invited and earnestly urged to send one, or more delegates to represent them in this, the tenth annual convention of the National Equal Rights League, and the Second National Congress of All Colored Americans. Without regard to creed, sect, sex or party, let us come together, and compel public attention to our demand for a "square deal" as citizens of this great republic.
